questions.specification:
The image compares a normal artery with one containing a plaque deposit. Which statement best explains how physical activity reduces the risk of the condition shown in the second artery?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Q4g1KiRFbX5QSGmV4129o)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.2 Benefits to health of being active > A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.3 Transport > A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It specifically concerns cardiovascular physiology and disease risk (arteries, cholesterol, endothelial function), fitting cardiovascular system function in transport.

options.content: It increases stroke volume, which reduces arterial elasticity.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Although physical activity can increase stroke volume, regular exercise is associated with improved (not reduced) arterial compliance/elasticity and does not reduce risk by decreasing elasticity.

options.content: It increases HDL cholesterol, which promotes reverse cholesterol transport and reduces plaque formation.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Regular physical activity can increase HDL cholesterol and support reverse cholesterol transport, but this statement does not best explain risk reduction compared with lowering LDL and improving endothelial function.

options.content: It lowers resting blood pressure, reducing stress on artery walls and slowing atherosclerosis.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is a plausible benefit of exercise, but the best explanation for reducing plaque-related risk here is reduced LDL cholesterol and improved endothelial function.

options.content: It decreases LDL cholesterol and enhances endothelial function.
options.markscheme: Correct. Regular physical activity can lower LDL cholesterol and improve endothelial function (e.g., increased nitric oxide availability), reducing atherosclerotic plaque development and cardiovascular risk.

Using the clinical data in the table, what is a primary health benefit associated with regular physical activity?

| Activity Level | Mean Systolic BP (mmHg) | Mean Diastolic BP (mmHg) | Relative Risk of Stroke |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Sedentary (0 min d⁻¹) | 142 | 94 | 1.00 |
| Moderate (30 min d⁻¹) | 130 | 85 | 0.74 |
| Vigorous (60 min d⁻¹) | 124 | 81 | 0.62 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.2 Benefits to health of being active > A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2.1 Active lifestyle and physical well-being

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3.1 The cardiovascular system and bodily functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting changes in systolic/diastolic blood pressure and cardiovascular risk (stroke) relates to cardiovascular system function and health outcomes.

options.content: Physical activity mainly reduces stroke risk and has little effect on blood pressure.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The table shows both systolic BP (142 to 130 to 124 mmHg) and diastolic BP (94 to 85 to 81 mmHg) decrease as activity level increases, alongside reduced relative risk of stroke.

options.content: Increased activity levels support cardiovascular health by reducing the chronic pressure exerted on arterial walls.
options.markscheme: Correct. Higher activity levels are associated with lower mean systolic and diastolic blood pressure values, indicating reduced chronic arterial pressure (and the table also shows a lower relative risk of stroke).

options.content: Only vigorous exercise is effective at lowering systolic blood pressure below the hypertensive threshold of 140 mmHg.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Moderate activity (30 min d⁻¹) is associated with a mean systolic BP of 130 mmHg, which is already below 140 mmHg; therefore it is not only vigorous exercise.

options.content: Doubling the daily duration of exercise results in a linear, two-fold decrease in the relative risk of stroke.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Increasing from 30 to 60 min d⁻¹ changes relative risk from 0.74 to 0.62, which is not a two-fold decrease and does not demonstrate linearity.
